summary: In this 1996 interview, Steve Jobs discusses his work at NeXT and Pixar, the future of the web as a dynamic e-commerce and distribution channel, and his reflections on Apple. He emphasizes the importance of object-oriented programming, the need for continuous innovation to maintain differentiation, and his philosophy of treating computer science as a liberal art.

principles.statement: Hire great people to tell you what to do, not the other way around.
principles.rationale: If you are paying top talent a lot of money, their job is to figure out the best ideas, which requires an egalitarian culture where ideas don't map to organizational hierarchy.
principles.source_quote: we hired people to tell us what to do. We figure we're paying them all this money you know their job is to figure out what to do and tell us

principles.statement: Non-standard products must be vastly superior to succeed.
principles.rationale: Consumers take a risk when buying outside the mainstream standard, so the product must offer a much bigger reward (50-100% better) rather than just a marginal improvement.
principles.source_quote: Apple has to be 50 or 100 percent better because when you buy something that is is you know out of the mainstream a little bit you take a risk and you want a much bigger reward for taking that risk

principles.statement: Computer science is a liberal art.
principles.rationale: Computers should not be relegated to a small percentage of geeks; they should be accessible, beautiful, and mastered by everyone to help them communicate.
principles.source_quote: science and computer science is a liberal art it's something that everyone should know how to use at least and harness in their life

mental_models.name: The Software Industrial Revolution
mental_models.description: Viewing object-oriented programming as the equivalent of the Industrial Revolution for software, shifting from custom-built everything to reusable components.
mental_models.example: Using object-oriented technology to build software 10 times faster by utilizing reusable parts, just as the Industrial Revolution did for hard goods.

mental_models.name: The Web as the Great Leveler
mental_models.description: Viewing the internet as a distribution channel that bypasses middlemen and allows small companies to appear as formidable as large ones.
mental_models.example: A small company investing heavily in their website to look better and more 'hip' than a large corporation, selling directly to consumers.

heuristics: Don't be afraid to fail; you can't achieve what you want without falling on your face a few times.
If you lose your differentiation, you must innovate your way out, not cost-cut your way out.

signature_quotes.text: you never achieve what you want without falling on your face a few times in the process of getting there
signature_quotes.context: Discussing his ouster from Apple and learning not to be afraid to fail.

signature_quotes.text: we hired people to tell us what to do. We figure we're paying them all this money you know their job is to figure out what to do and tell us
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the bottoms-up, egalitarian corporate culture at Apple.

signature_quotes.text: I think our major contribution was in bringing a liberal arts point of view to the use of computers
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on Apple's legacy and the motivation behind the Macintosh's ease of use and typography.

signature_quotes.text: the cure for apple is to innovate its way out of its current predicament
signature_quotes.context: Commenting on Apple's struggles in 1996 and why cost-cutting wouldn't save the company.

anti_patterns.description: Copying the paradigms of your past success.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The world changes underneath your feet, and the door to establish the same kind of company or product may have already closed.

anti_patterns.description: Mapping the hierarchy of ideas to the hierarchy of the organization.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Great ideas can come from anywhere, and a strict top-down hierarchy stifles the great people you hired to figure things out.

anti_patterns.description: Stopping innovation after gaining a massive lead.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Your differentiation evaporates, making you a 'sitting duck' for competitors to catch up over time.
